task_name: N/A - Educational presentation, not an FLS task performance
content_description: SAGES 2024 Education Short Oral Session: Laparoscopic Pancreatoduodenectomy - Skill Development and Clinical Impact Through Simulation Training
moderators: Jonathan Dort, MD & Neal Seymour, MD

key_points: Laparoscopic pancreatoduodenectomy (LPD) offers clinically significant advantages over open approach when performed by expert surgeons
Reduction in postoperative complications and conversion rates after 25-84 cases, mainly due to reconstructive phase
Simulation training may help shorten learning curve
Images of simulated foam appendage models shown

presentation_structure.content: Describe 7-year experience of laparoscopic simulation course in hepatopancreatobiliary surgery and infer clinical usefulness

key_points: Participants: trainees completing Hepatobiliary and Pancreatic Surgery Skills Course (HPB-C) between 2017-2023
Course encompassed 25 stages simulating choledochojejunal (CJ) and pancreaticojejunal (PJ) anastomosis using synthetic and ex-vivo tissues
Pre/post assessments: Global Rating Scale (GRS; 5-25 pts), Specific Rating Scale (SRS; 4-20 pts), procedural time, leakage rates
Online survey to assess impact on trainee experience, exposure, and confidence
Significant improvements in GRS and SRS (p-values < 0.0001)
Pancreaticojejunostomy: Similar improvements across both scales
Significant reduction in procedural time: CJ anastomosis 27 min → 18 min; PJ anastomosis 36 min → 21 min
Leakage rates decreased for both procedures
20 previous trainees completed survey (40% response rate)
85% increased confidence in laparoscopic hepatobiliary procedures
60% reported increased exposure to digestive and hepatobiliary laparoscopic procedures

presentation_structure.content: HPB surgery simulation program demonstrated efficacy in enhancing surgical skills within controlled environment; survey responses emphasized positive influence on participants' surgical capabilities and importance of structured simulation-based training in hepatopancreatobiliary surgery

assessment_note: This is an educational presentation/scientific abstract at a medical conference (SAGES 2024). It does NOT contain a video performance of an FLS task (Peg Transfer, Pattern Cut, Endoloop, Extracorporeal Suture, or Intracorporeal Suture). Therefore, no FLS scoring rubric applies. The content discusses the design, methods, and results of a surgical simulation training program, but does not show actual task performance by a trainee or expert to be scored.
reason: This is instructional/educational content presenting research findings on simulation training. Per classification rules, instructional videos with narration, slides, and lecture content are not scored using FLS rubrics; only actual task performances are scored.
